reason: The item cleanly tests boundary-aware admissibility: separating measurable heating from true propagation and linking multi-source ISC to a threshold-type implication, while also checking disciplined claims about dominant heat-transfer mode. Decoys are mostly single-fault and engineering-plausible (temperature-rise-to-propagation conflation, mechanism over-commitment, and ignoring radiation–conduction tradeoffs), yielding good expert discrimination. Some options (notably E) can feel borderline because it gestures at cumulative heat input and could be seen as partially aligned, reducing separation strength slightly.

The item cleanly targets a boundary-aware mechanistic distinction: dominant radiative transfer does not imply sufficient energy delivery to cross the neighbor’s exothermic reaction activation threshold, and it separately tests admissibility of a “prevents propagation” claim given only single-cell evidence. It has strong discriminative power because it penalizes the common overreach of equating reduced peak temperature/weight advantage with demonstrated propagation prevention, while keeping the reasoning axis coherent and technically grounded.
